topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee mug felt like a weapon in my hand, the ceramic warm against my palm, but the heat wasn't enough to quell the churning in my gut. I stared at Mark, his easy smile directed at Sarah, and the world narrowed down to the space between us. "You really don't remember," I managed, the words scraping out of my throat, sounding brittle and sharp. My jaw was tight enough to crack enamel. I watched his eyes flicker, a momentary blankness before he offered a vague, dismissive shrug. Every muscle in my shoulders tensed, a rigid knot of frustration tightening around my neck. I wanted to shout, to shatter the polite veneer of this interaction, to make him *see* the years we shared, the secrets we buried under the old oak tree. I took a sharp, shallow breath, trying to force the tremor out of my hands, but the tension vibrated through my entire frame.

[story 2]

I slammed the door shut, the sound echoing in the small apartment like a gunshot. My hands were shaking so badly I had to press them against my thighs to steady them. It wasn't just disappointment; it was a white-hot surge of incredulity that burned behind my eyes. How could he just… erase me? I paced the length of the living room, my footsteps heavy and uneven on the hardwood floor. "That's impossible," I muttered to the empty air, my voice low and vibrating with suppressed energy. I stopped abruptly by the window, staring out at the indifferent city lights. A strange, hot pressure built behind my sternum, a desperate need to confront him, to force a reaction, any reaction, that acknowledged the depth of what I felt was being dismissed. I clenched my fists until my knuckles were stark white, the sheer effort of holding back the urge to scream making my breath hitch in ragged gasps.

[story 3]

When he finally spoke, his tone was infuriatingly casual, as if discussing the weather rather than dismantling a decade of shared history. I leaned forward across the table, resting my elbows on the wood, my gaze locked onto his. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the table, the pressure radiating outward. "You don't even recall the summer we spent building that ridiculous fort?" The question hung in the air, laced with a bitter edge that I couldn't quite contain. I watched his face shift, a flicker of confusion crossing it, quickly replaced by that infuriatingly blank expression. A deep, internal clenching occurred in my abdomen, a tightening that made my stomach revolt. I wanted to physically shake him, to demand that he access the memory banks he claimed were inaccessible. I gripped the edge of my own chair until my fingers ached, a silent, desperate display of the sheer, unadulterated feeling of being utterly invalidated.
